artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching up + why I keep talking about squad cars

Hey Steve,

Finally have a quiet Sunday afternoon after running Jacob to hockey and helping Mom sort through another stack of Essentia bills. She’s still got about $48,000 hanging over her from Dad’s ICU stay at Essentia–St. Joseph’s before he died in March 2022. She won’t say it out loud, but I can tell it scares her. I keep thinking about those talks with him in the ICU when he grabbed my hand and told me, “Make sure your guys have better cars than we did.” That’s stuck with me.

It’s part of why I’m pushing so hard to keep the 2026 hybrid squad purchase inside that $132,500 cap and on schedule. After that July 2022 arbitration at the Crow Wing County Courthouse over Miller’s unsafe vehicle grievance, when the union attorney read my own handwritten note about the 2015 squad dying on us in the Brainerd Ice Arena lot with Jacob in the back seat, I swore I’d never let our family stories end up in an evidence file again.

You remember that night—subzero, transmission gives out, we’re freezing in our gear while I wait for a tow and try to pretend to Jacob it’s all “part of the job.” Hearing that replayed in a courtroom was brutal.

Day-to-day life is otherwise pretty normal: Emily’s buried in college apps, Jacob’s living at the rink, and Laura and I are just trying to keep everyone fed and mostly on time. Let’s try for an evening on Gull Lake soon—no talk of budgets, just walleyes and Dad stories.

Mike

metadata.subject: Catching up + why I keep talking about squad cars

artifacts.content:
Subject: Rough night with 271

Laura,

I’m still a little wound up from last night, so I’m typing this before I forget details. On January 16, we had a domestic on Holly Street involving the same family from that October 2023 child-protection case Crow Wing County Human Services is still reviewing. Unit 271’s electrical system crapped out on the way, and we rolled up about six minutes later than we should have.

Nothing blew up, thank God, but it easily could’ve turned into a serious use-of-force situation. I keep replaying it and thinking how I’d explain to that CPS worker—or a judge—why we were late because we’re still limping along in a 2017 squad.

It’s the same feeling I had back on January 7 when Ruiz got stuck on Highway 210 at –18°F after 271 died again. He waited almost 25 minutes for a tow. Essentia–Brainerd’s ER note actually says “mild frostbite” on his fingers. I’ve already sent HR (Karen Olson) an incident summary and a workers’ comp note flagging the electrical history. Completely preventable if we had newer units.

This is also why I’m so stubborn about getting the hybrids in 2026. Between my own cardiology consult at Brainerd Lakes Heart Center last January—where they said the long overnight shifts sitting in an idling squad aren’t helping my blood pressure—and nights like last night, it feels like the job is literally wearing us down.

I’ll be late again tonight; we’re short a car, so I’m covering part of second shift. Try not to let Emily read too much into the sirens.

Love,
Mike

Title: Personal reflections – fleet, family, and overtime

I need this in one place so I remember what’s really driving some of my decisions.

1) Holly Street 1/16/25 – Unit 271 dies en route to domestic, same family as Oct 2023 CPS case. Six‑minute delay that could’ve gone sideways. Not something I’ll ever put in a public briefing, but it’s in my head every time I look at the maintenance log.

2) Ruiz frostbite 1/7/25 – 271 again, Highway 210 at –18°F, 25‑minute wait for a tow, mild frostbite per Essentia–Brainerd ER. Documented with HR (Karen Olson) and workers’ comp, but the part that sticks with me is Dan trying to warm his hands on a dead steering wheel.

3) July 2022 arbitration at Crow Wing County Courthouse – union attorney reading my handwritten note about the 2015 squad that stranded Jacob and me outside the Brainerd Ice Arena in subzero temps. Hearing our family moment turned into evidence was a gut punch.

4) My own health – January 2024 cardiology consult at Brainerd Lakes Heart Center after Dr. Schultz flagged the BP and cholesterol. Clear message: less time sitting in an idling car, more walking, more sleep. Hard to do when we’re constantly shorthanded and babysitting dying squads.

Bottom line: replacing 271/272 on schedule isn’t just about line items. It’s about not having my officers, or my kids, or my mom show up in another grievance file because we squeezed a few extra miles out of a car.

metadata.title: Personal reflections – fleet, family, and overtime

artifacts.content:
Subject: Emily and the sirens

Mrs. Larson,

I just wanted to say thank you again for the time you’ve been spending with Emily since the March 2023 fatal pursuit that ended near the Highway 25 bridge. She doesn’t always talk to me directly, but she tells Laura little pieces of what you work on.

She mentioned that she still gets panic attacks when she hears sirens, and that one of her fears is that my “old squad will fail” if I’m ever in another chase. That one stung, because she’s not wrong about the age of some of our units.

We’re scheduled to replace two of the 2017 frontline squads with 2026 Ford hybrid Interceptors as part of the capital plan. When that’s approved, I plan to physically show her the new cars—explain the upgrades, the maintenance schedule, and that we’re doing everything we can so the equipment isn’t the weak link. I know that doesn’t fix the trauma from seeing the aftermath of that Highway 25 incident, but I’m hoping it gives her something concrete to hold on to.

On a personal note, I’m also trying to follow my own doctor’s advice. I had a cardiology consult at Brainerd Lakes Heart Center last January after my primary, Dr. Schultz at Essentia, flagged my blood pressure. The cardiologist specifically called out the long overnight shifts sitting in an idling squad. Moving to hybrids and getting out of the car more is one of the few changes I can realistically make.

Thanks again for looking out for her.

Sincerely,
Chief Michael Jensen

Title: Fleet/health/counseling – personal planning block

Time: Thursday, 2025-05-08, 7:00–7:45 a.m.
Location: Kitchen table, 1429 Maple Ridge Dr.

Notes:

Use this quiet window before shift to get my head straight on a few intertwined things:

– Family counseling at Northern Pines: Weekly Thursdays 5:30–6:30 p.m. with Laura and Jacob since May 2, 2024 (anxiety and truancy at Brainerd High). Try to avoid signing up for OT that bumps into this. If squads keep dying and I’m covering more late shifts, those sessions are the first thing to get squeezed.

– Tax situation: Since January 2024 I’ve been sending the IRS an extra $400/month to clear the $7,800 underpayment from the 2022 off‑duty security work at the Brainerd Lakes Event Center. I’m counting on some overtime in 2026 to finish that by December 2025. If the hybrid purchase goes over the $132,500 cap and Finance clamps down on OT, that plan gets shaky.

– Mom’s medical costs: Carol still on the hook for about $48,000 from Dad’s Essentia–St. Joseph’s ICU stay before he died in March 2022. I don’t want any hint that my push for new squads is about my salary or benefits; for her sake, it has to stay clearly about officer safety.

– My health: Remember January 2024 cardiology consult at Brainerd Lakes Heart Center—less time in idling squads, more walking. Hybrids help a little.

Bring this mindset into today’s conversations so I don’t lose the forest for the trees.

Thread with Laura – insurance + old squads

Mike: Sitting in the car outside Essentia waiting for Mom’s latest lab work. Been thinking about our insurance mess since Emily’s fender‑bender by Brainerd High last May.

Laura: You and me both. Northland Mutual still has us at that $1,000 collision deductible. I hate it.

Mike: Same. If council forces us to run the 2017 squads another 2–3 years instead of funding the hybrids, I don’t feel great about dropping that deductible. Too much risk of another on‑duty crash in an old unit.

Laura: We’re already juggling Mom’s stroke rehab payments and Jacob’s gear. I don’t want another surprise bill.

Mike: Exactly. Between Mom’s stroke rehab at Essentia–St. Joe’s last March (we’re still sending them $650/month), the $2,300 we ate on my PT and chiro at Cuyuna after that rear‑end on Oak Street in April, and the $400/month I’m sending the IRS to clear the $7,800 from the 2022 Brainerd Lakes Event Center gigs, we’re stretched thin.

Laura: Which is why I get nervous when you talk about covering more OT if the fleet keeps breaking.

Mike: That’s the irony. I need the OT to pay the IRS off by Dec 2025, but if the squad purchase blows past the $132,500 cap, Finance will probably squeeze overtime. Feels like we’re betting our own budget on whether 271 and 272 finally die.

Laura: One day at a time. Let’s just make sure Emily gets through this week without another panic about your car.

Mike: Agreed. I’ll be home by 7 if the lab doesn’t run too far behind.

Subject: Long day – Mom, squads, and that old arbitration

Rachel,

Just got home from Good Sam after checking on Mom and wanted to get a few things off my chest before I crash.

Mom had another confused spell this afternoon, nothing like the September 3 episode when we had to get her from the Buffalo Hills Lane assisted‑living place to Essentia–St. Joseph’s for that emergency dementia evaluation, but enough that I’m watching her more closely. That was also the night my unit stalled on Pine Shores Road after too much idling. Sitting there with her in the back while we waited for a jump was one of the more embarrassing moments of my career.

It brought me right back to that July 2022 arbitration at the Crow Wing County Courthouse over Miller’s unsafe vehicle grievance. The union’s attorney read my handwritten note about the 2015 squad dying in the Brainerd Ice Arena parking lot after Jacob’s hockey practice—subzero, transmission failure, Jacob shivering in the back seat. Hearing that in a courtroom, with my kid’s name in the transcript, was awful.

Since then, I’ve been more stubborn about staying on our replacement schedule. We’ve already had Ruiz’s frostbite on Highway 210 when 271 died at –18°, and the near‑miss on Holly Street when 271’s electrical system failed en route to that domestic tied to the October 2023 CPS case. I keep thinking: we’re one bad night away from another arbitration file with our family in it.

Anyway, on the home front Emily’s plugging away at college essays, Jacob’s hockey bag still smells like something died in it, and Laura’s on early shifts this week. Tell Noah Uncle Mike says to keep his stick on the ice.

Mike

metadata.subject: Long day – Mom, squads, and that old arbitration
